title: 2025/12/04 - TI (Part 1)
displayTitle: 2025-12-03 - Transport and Infrastructure Committee - 2024/25 Annual review of the Ministry of Transport [Part 1]
topic: 2024/25 Annual review of the Ministry of Transport
description: Thursday 4 December 2025 | Transport and Infrastructure Committee 2024/25 Annual review of the Ministry of Transport Hearing of evidence: Hon Chris Bishop, Minister of Transport Hon James Meager, Associate Minister of Transport Ministry of Transport Ruth Fairhall, Acting Secretary for Transport and Chief Executive David Wood, Deputy Chief Executive, Investment and Monitoring Paul O’Connell, Deputy Chief Executive, Sector Strategy Carmen Mak, Deputy Chief Executive, Corporate Services Siobhan Routledge, Acting Deputy Chief Executive, Policy Chris Nees, Acting Chief of Staff Penelope Leith, Chief Financial Officer NZ Transport Agency Waka Kotahi Brett Gliddon, Chief Executive
descriptionSummary: 2024/25 Annual review of the Ministry of Transport. Hon Chris Bishop, Minister of Transport. Hon James Meager, Associate Minister of Transport

chunks.text: ... you're an army.

All right. Well, welcome. Um, we've got you here for, um, half an hour. We've got, I think we, we've got a minute bonus because we're early. (laughs) Um, so we've got you here for half an hour, so, um, the usual process will, um... You know, if you want to save about three or four minutes to start off with, and then we'll go straight to questions. I don't know if you... How do you want to handle it between the two of you in terms of- Well, we've got delegations, so anything- Yeah.

Would you-... delegation, anything like that. Do you both want to do some intros in your own areas and then- Uh, we- we don't have a lot to say. I mean- Okay. Um- Right. Okay, anyway, in your heads. Well, uh, just, just a couple of things that I think would be worth maybe thinking about. Um, one is, uh, transition to road user charges, electronic road user charges, um, which is a, a, a really important structural change for the revenue system that runs the transport system obviously. Um, the simple reality is we have, uh, declining revenue over

time because of the rise of hybrids and EVs, um, and fuel efficiency, which is a good, which is a good thing, um, but we've got to make sure that, uh, the revenue... we get the revenue to run the, run the roads and the transport system. And so, the electronic road user charges is, is a really important, um, step towards revenue sustainability, uh, but also recognizing the fact that the RUC system, you know, needs to enter the modern world as well.

I mean, it's pretty ridiculous you still need to go out and buy your $1000 license or your 1000 kilometer license. It gets sent in the mail and you've got to put it on the front s- stick, you know, in the front, front of your dashboard and all the rest of it. So, we're trying to bring... I mean, the RUC system itself is actually world-leading in comparison to a lot of other countries where we're far in advance of other places, but we've got to bring it into the modern world.

The other point I'd stress is that we're not, we're not rushing this. Um, there's, there's quite a bit of work to do. Um, uh, and, you know, we'll be ma- we'll be making decisions, um, in d- in due course. Um, and successive governments will have to do that as well. And so, we're quite keen to do this in a quite constructive and sensible way. Um, and yeah, so I, you know, I encourage the committee to have a, have a think about that, um, in the future. But, um, there's a lot of other things.

I could talk for half an hour, but obviously it's better if you have questions, so happy to, happy to go straight in. Yeah. I just think you'd, you'd like to say as well? Yeah. It's the only thing I would add is that, um, in the aviation space is a real focus on regional connectivity and the work that, um, ministry is doing in that space, along with, uh, airlines, airports and civil aviation. Uh, rules reform, certification processes, the Cross-Industry Aviation Action Plan and we acknowledge the previous government for the Interim Aviation Council bringing

together industry and government for the first time. And then in the maritime space, significant investment in and around Chatham Islands shipping, vessel, uh, and coastal shipping services as well. So our focus really is on the connectivity, logistics and infrastructure. (coughs) Wow. Making sure our rules are fit for purpose and, um, the committee would have seen some rules announced last week around advanced aviation and just being an enabler in that space as well. That's really it.

Cool. Okay. We'll go straight to questions. I'm gonna take, uh, Tangi and then- Oh, Yeah. Thanks, mate. (clears throat) Thank you. And if nobody else goes, I'll go next. Um- Thank you, Mr. Chair. Um, thank you, ministers. Um, I wanted to ask questions about RONS. Um, I think Minister, you, you have now publicly acknowledged that the figure associated with delivering the RONS at this stage will be around $56 billion, which is quite a significant increase on what had previously been signaled when, uh, when you were identifying this. And I note that you said that there are hard

choices lie ahead because of the, the challenges. In terms of actually, um, transitioning that project to, um, delivering, I think the amount that's been set aside is about $1.2 billion, which is basically around 2% of that, that total. So h- how, how can this credibly still be, uh, delivered as a suite, uh, of, of a package, uh, given the real funding constraints that you yourself now identify?

Yeah, I think that's a good question. Um, the, the roads of national significance and indeed, um, I tend to talk about major transport projects in the round because it's not just about the roads. Um, we've got significant, uh, demand for important projects like the North Western Busway in Auckland, for example, which is a very important project.

Obviously public transport in Auckland, Wellington and Christchurch and other places as well. Um, so you're right that there's a, there's a, uh, that they're expensive. Um, and what I was trying to lay out in that speech that I think you're referencing, uh, a couple of weeks ago is that, um, there, there are real challenges around that.

Um, what, what I hear from the industry, uh, and, and, and also New Zealanders is they want a credible pipeline of delivery in the long term. So I'm just... You know, I was just stating the obvious in a way a couple of weeks ago, which is that we're not gonna build everything straight away and, um, not everything can happen all at the same time.

So the idea you'd go out and have six major roads in construction, you just literally could not do that from a market capacity point of view. And if you did do it, it would push up construction cost inflation to stratospheric levels, so you, you actually don't want to do that. What you want is a, is a credible, long term, sustainable pipeline that people can see ahead of them.

Um, and so we're doing some work on that at the moment. Um, we're not, we're not there yet, but that's a, that's a 2026 type thing. And, and related to that, um, again just trying to make the point to people so everyone's aware of it, that there are quite difficult choices around that. Ultimately, roads ha- roads and public transport projects have to be paid for. Now, fuel tax, um, is going up in 2027 and 2028, um, but-... but it has been held flat for quite a long time, um, and, you know, there's a... Fuel tax will have to go up, but there's also a limit,

economically and politically, as I think you would acknowledge, to how much fuel tax can go up. Um, tolls are a useful revenue source, um, crown capital contributions into the National Land Transport Fund, which successive governments have been making, uh, for- for some time now. Um, that- that capital is contestable with roads and- w- with our hospitals and schools and defense. Committee will be aware of significant, um, uplift in defense capital. Uh, so all of that- all of that competes.

And the roa- the- the transport system, which is meant to be self-funding through- through RUC and fuel tax, has not been for- for a long time, um, and so I'm- I'm just ma- I'm just trying to s- speak the blunt reality, um, and, um, you know, it's good that you noticed (laughs) . I do as well. That's- that's accepted. Um, in terms of that reference to, you know, work being done at the moment but not available yet. So, I infer from that that there is a reprioritization process that you're looking at in terms of the suite of RONS and which ones might be able to be delivered

at what point of time. Is that what you're referencing there? Y- y- more sequencing. So we're committed to the- the- to the 17 RONS that are in the GPS, the- the Government Policy Statement. You know, those are- those are government priorities as- as they're reflected in the GPS, but they're at different stages, right? So, just take two obvious examples, um, and- and don't- and don't infer anything from this, um, in terms of the prioritization, but- but it just illustrates the point.

Cambridge to Piary, which is the extension of the Waikato Expressway, you know, that just got consent through- through the previous government's fast track, uh, process, um, so 20- 20- 24 kilometers? Is that right? 21? Roughly. 20... Yeah, ro- yeah, let's say 20, offline extension of the Waikato Expressway. Um, that- that's got consents.

That's been- look, that's been planned for years, you know? There's been various different commitments made around that. That is- that is quite close to- well, close-ish to construction. Uh, the North West Alternative State Highway in North West Auckland, you know, that- that's frankly just like a line on a map. That- that is- that is like years away from construction.

So, that- the kind of my point is that- that they are actually at different stages anyway, um, so some are closer to- construction, s- some are- are much further down- down the line and that- and that's why that 1.2 billion that you referenced, which sounds like a big number but actually when you- when you add it all up around the different projects, you know, it all- it all sort of evens out.

That is- that is to get projects further up the kind of food chain, so to speak, towards construction- construction readiness. So it's about, you know, designations and land acquisition and consenting and route protection and doing the designs. I mean, all of that stuff takes time and- and costs money, and, um, you know, it's not quite as simple as maybe some members of the public think. It's like, "Oh yeah, we're gonna build a road here, just turn up and build it." Like it's- it's much more complicated than that, as I think the committee will

appreciate. So, so that's- that's- that's- that 1.2 billion is going towards creating that credible pipeline. And in terms of to your question about prioritization, it's not so much about reprioritization. I would classify it as, um, trying to get the right sequence, um, and- and the right time frames.

Just one- One more, yep, and then over to you again. ... further brief sub. Given that your government has, uh, sort of presided over cuts in the areas of road safety, public transport and the like- I just got to go to here.... what guarantee is there that as these projects move forward, not prioritization but sequencing, that communities will not face even deeper cuts as a result to find the revenue to- No, no.

... to- to sort out? I don't know what you mean by- I don't know what you mean by cuts to road safety and public transport. There's more money for both in this NLTP period. In fact, we've made quite a significant investment in Road Police and road safety, for example. So, what's that a reference to? Well- well, in terms of road safety, the focus on road safety is not a priority and likewise, if you, you know, talk to any council around the country, they'll talk to you about the need that they actually have to do much of

the heavy lifting now in terms of farebox recovery to- to initiate an increase in revenue. It is- it is- So you having said- Just let me finish. Sorry. So having said that, what guarantees can you provide to communities to ensure that there are no further deeper cuts as a result of needing to find the revenue to- Yeah. Well, we- we can disagree. I mean, I reject your characterization of cuts, we- but we can agree to disagree on that. Um, we- we have asked, um, p- public transport authorities to lift, um, their private share, um,

or- or to lift their private share, um, and that- and that's because, uh, there's been a quite steep decline in- in the amount of private share that people contribute towards public transport, um, o- over time. It sort of started- actually started a little bit pre-COVID, but it sort of accelerated during COVID. Um, and so we're trying to get- trying to get that share back to what it was pre kind of 2019. Um, w- we haven't actually set that target to... L- like, the- the numbers actually won't even get back to pre-2019 levels.

We've just- we've just asked for a bit more. But- but also NZTA is taking a flexible approach with- with public transport authorities, um, around that. Um, so I- I reject- I reject your characterization, um, but you're right. The- the roads and- and major transport projects, they kind of have... I mean, there's a baseline level of things you have to do right in the system, um, with transport system funding from... And maintenance is obviously critical and I think you probably had InfraCom in already and they'll have told you that, you know, as a country, we've

been far too focused on new stuff at the expense of looking after what we've already got. That's important. And there's big investment in this, um, GPS period, uh, into road maintenance and, significant uplift there which is really important. It's making a difference around the country. So you got your baseline level stuff, and I would classify public transport as part of that, and, um, road safety, uh, all the different things that are in the activity classes, and then you got your stuff on top of that, which is your new-

your new spend. And, you know, we'll have a Government Policy Statement in 2027, uh, as the next kind of-... you know, it's the next window to, to look at that stuff. And, um, you know, as I say, there, there are some quite tough choices because, um, every, you know... And council's a part of this too. Everyone wants something, and you gotta work out where the money goes. (laughs) Um, the colleagues, um, rather than take subs on these, 'cause we've only got 20 more minutes, um, what I'm gonna do is just, so put your subs into your main bits.

If we can get round, 'cause everybody wants to have a say at some point, so we'll go do that, and if, uh, we got some time for some subs on the end of that, we'll do that. So Julianne, now I'm gonna come over here to- Could the minister-... Can I just ask, mister, through the chair- Oh, yeah. ... if the minister could be a little more succinct in his answers, we could ask more questions? Well- Um, if- that'd be awesome. We, we, we will try. You're getting very full some answers, but- (laughs) Yeah.

We'll try and- Trying to-... trying to keep it all tight. Trying to- Mine... Happily, mine are very much in the same area. I'm a helpful man, Julianne. I'm just trying to assist you- Okay, come on.... on your journey. Let's go. Um, uh, my area of questions was related to Tangi, so, um, I wanted to know will... How important is, uh, benefit-cost analysis in determining the prioritization of the RONZ?

Uh, it's- And the sequencing. It's important, but it's, um, it's, it's not the determinative factor. Um, why not? Well, because, uh, you, you, we're, we're trying to take a range of things into account in terms of the sequencing. Um, so if you did a, if you did a strict, uh, if you did it strictly on BCR, right, leaving aside the, the merits or otherwise of, of benefit-costs, which, you know, has its own issues.

If you did it strictly on BCR, you might end up with quite a skewed, um, sequence. Um- Right. But if something is low benefits relative to cost, is it likely to come early on in the program of RONZ? I suppose what I would say to you is we are taking account of a range of things, benefit-cost is part of it. Um, regional spread, because you don't... Like, you, you don't wanna end up in the situation where, you know, and I'm not saying this would happen be- on a strict BCR basis, but y- if you just...

All your projects were in Auckland, for example, that would not be, um, economically sustainable for Auckland, but also frankly wouldn't be politically sustainable for everybody. Um, and, uh, um, i- i- it also, uh, y- we're taking account of m- readiness for market and construction readiness as well, because, you know, again, if you talk to the sector, they'll say to you, "We've gotta stop the boom-bust, up-down, yo-yo effect." And so what we're trying to do is, and this is why it's complicated, is, is get a credible 10 to 20-year sequence of major

projects around the country, um, that is, that is deliverable, um, has a regional spread, so that, so that there's a good spread around the country and you're not focused or concentrated on one area, um, and that are good value-for-money projects. And, you know, there are clearly, there's, like, clearly tensions between all those things, which is why it's complicated, which is why we haven't done it yet.

(laughs) Should, um- (clears throat)... congestion pricing or changes to pricing, like tolling, be taken into account early on in determining the pr- like, projects that might happen? Yeah. We're doing, we're doing some thinking about that. The, the challenge, of course, is that we don't have any congestion pricing schemes established yet, and obviously we've just passed the legislation and thank you to, to your committee for all your work on that.

Uh, but a- and Auckland's working away with officials now on, on getting, getting, um-... some stuff, some stuff ready for 2026, 2027. But that's a, that's a bit down the line. Um, so we're do- just doing some thinking about how we might integrate, uh, that policy, which I think everyone kind of supports broadly into, into that work. But it, it has previously been taken into account in modeling demand, benefits, costs of projects prior, so could it- Yeah, it could be- There's nothing stopping it from being No, no. And this is why we're doing some thinking about it.

Yeah. It, it can be, it can be looked at, but, but also I don't wanna get ahead of where Auckland or, or maybe Wellington gets to. This is one more. You know what I Yeah. Um, the changes to the discount rate and evaluation period have, uh, substantial impacts on the overall benefits and costs of, um, the RONZ. So you've extended the evaluation period to 60 years, and the discount rate's been lowered to 2% or even 1.5 far out in the future. The Ministry of Transport came in and briefed us on their long-term insights briefing and said it was extremely

difficult and basically impossible to model, um, travel demand 60 years in the future and even 30 years in the future. They said it was difficult. Actually, my main question is- (laughs) I mean, you're right. I didn't do the VCR thing, by the way. Is, is the- Treasury did that. Yeah, sure. Well, is, is NZTA... I mean, is the intention for Ministry of Transport, NZTA and local authorities to now use the same lower discount rate and longer evaluation period across all projects?

That is a good question. I don't know. I mean, I, I'm not responsible for the discount rate thing. Treasury did that. Um, Ruth, do you or Brett, do you... What... Do you know what the rule, what the rule is there? We can come back this afternoon and get Can we put that on notice and come back? Okay. Do, do- I actually don't know what the answer to that is. It's a good question. Okay. Um, I mean, you're right about the 50 and 60 year thing. I mean, like, you know, the... I mean, who knows what world we'll be like in

2075? I mean, flying cars, flying planes, so. (laughs) Everybody wouldn't be building roads till it's ... I mean- (laughs) Lots of airports. Well, yeah. (laughs) (laughs) So. Thank you, Mr. Chair. Um, Minister, just interested in exploring a little bit more about the opportunities that, uh, that the RONZ project offers- Mm-hmm.

Uh, or the program. I mean, you've been very forthright in explaining, uh, the cost issues, the timing issues, um, the capacity that's gonna constrain New Zealand's ability to deliver those projects. But I'm interested in whether, uh, w- whether, uh, you've considered the RONZ specification, the four-laning- Yep. ... grade-separated interchanges, very wide lanes- Yep.

... uh, is actually necessary to deliver- Yeah.... the benefits? Yeah. Because when I look at Australia, even on the ends- S- Simon, we'll keep the, keep the questions tight, please. Yeah, yeah. Well, M- Mr. Chair, I'd just like to explain the context. Uh, I th- I think Simon's In Australia, they have, um, they're quite open to 80 to 110 is the range. Yeah.

Some wi- some two-lane, some three-lane- Yeah. ... some four-lane. Is that something you've considered? Y- yes, it is. And, uh, th- to be honest, we've actually moved away from the kind of 100 to 110 four-lane, you know, gold-plated expressways already. So, um-Just take a local example that I happen to know a little bit about, um, Petone to Granada for example, where you down to 60 to 80 there?

60 now- S- it's 60 to 80, and there's two... is it four lanes or two? No, it's four lanes but it's 60 to 80. Four lanes but it's two... but it's 60 to 80, so it's... that... so that means the gradient is lower, right? So it's significantly cheaper, cuz yeah. So, um... and there's a... and actually if you read all the investment cases that... I mean, you could go through this with NZTA if you want later, but there's a range of different things. So kind of have done that. One of, one of my...

I'll just be honest with you, one of my frustrations in this is that, uh, it, it's just the high costs and, um, I have s- made it really clear to the Transport Agency, um, that I want value for money in terms of delivery and, um, we d- we do not... we do not need to gold plate things.

Um, we actually just need functional value-for-money roads, um, and where there's the ability to strip cost out, we should do that. Um, speed is part of that, but, um, there's, there's a ra- there... like, there's a range of different things. I mean, to be honest, the RMA reforms that, um, you know, I know you know a little bit about, Mr. Cork, uh, that will make a difference.

Like some of the consent conditions that get attached to roading projects drive significant cost, um, that just flows through to someone paying. Uh, and so that... the... all of these things make a difference, but, uh, to be honest, it's an enduring frustration for me, um, and we are not there yet and I am open to all sensible ideas as to how to strip cost out.

Um, I know the committee's done a little bit of work on this in the last few years already, um, and so we, we welcome your, your feedback on that. Thank you. Oh, my question is to the Associate Minister of Transport. Go back. Oh, very good. And he will shut up. Oh, very good. So we know that people are very frustrated at flight being canceled all the time. What, what have you done to address these issues?

That's a good question. Um, yeah, people are frustrated and especially those in the regions. So, earlier this year, we announced, um, some funding from the Regional Infrastructure Fund for regional air connectivity, so that's to enable, uh, regional airlines to invest in their own infrastructure, um, debt recovery, leasing arrangements to provide some securities for some of those regional routes.

Um, we've, we've invested in inter-lining arrangements, the technology to support those, and the first one of those launched between, um, Air Chathams and Air New Zealand in Whakatāne earlier this year and is actually d- due to go online probably this week, this month. And there's a, there's an ability to expand that, so then you, um, as a regional customer, you're buying one ticket to get from Whakatāne to Auckland to Wellington to Timaru say, rather than having to go through two or three airlines.

So those are probably a couple of initial steps and then there's broader Aviation Action Plan which looks at the industry, um, and the sector as a whole and it's looking at things like T- Minister Bishop's other portfolio around infrastructure. What are the long-term through-the-year infrastructure needs for airports and what does, um, aviation and air connectivity look like across the country?

Have you looked into the workforce issues? Yeah, so workforce is another one. We have a shortage of pilots and engineers and also, um, it can be a challenge to find space for our air traffic controllers in the system. Uh, again, the Aviation Action Plan puts a lot of focus on workforce development and, um, there is a report due back from Ringa Hora, uh, towards the end of the year about how we could possibly amend the pilot qualification system to speed up the training but also what can we do to, um, make more hours available to pilots because one of the, one of the barriers

to becoming a commercial pilot is that you can get your ticket and you do your 200 hours but then to be able to fly, you actually need five to six hundred hours and where do you get those hours in New Zealand? Um, a lot of people will choose to do that, um, uh, in their private time while they're working outside the sector and others may be forced to go overseas to get those hours. So we're looking at those kind of issues as well.

Sure. Thank you. Tracey. Mm-hmm. Also on the, uh, area of skilled New Zealand, skilled New Zealand, um, workers. Um, Minister wholesome is seeking to replace the New Zealand-flagged, um, MV Buffalo with cheaper foreign-flagged vessel which, as you'll be aware, puts 32, um, skilled Kiwi seaf- seafarers out of work just after Christmas, three days after Christmas. Um, we would contend that approving this undermines our maritime law, um, eh, coastal shipping capabilities and actually quite frankly the government's own, um, promise to put New Zealand workers

first. Uh, it's a bit of a test therefore of, uh, whether Kiwi jobs are more than just a slogan. Have you... where are you up to in that process? Have you... um, has the exemption application been approved? No, the application hasn't. The decision on the application hasn't been made yet. Um, it's still currently being considered, um, can't say too much more on that 'cause it is under consideration but I'm expecting a decision to be made in the next, um, next week or so.

And are those things that you're taking... those, those issues that I've prefaced, are those all issues that you're very aware of and, um, taking into account in terms of making that? Y- yes, that would be a fair summary. We've got to make the decision in line with what the statute requires us in the, the relevant considerations. Um, but those are certainly factors that I'm aware of both as Minister but also, um, you know, my role as a local member of Parliament. We're all...

those of us who have votes are all, um, very much, uh, enthusiastic about our seafaring industry being very, very strong so it's something we support. Thank you. Dan. Thanks, Mr. Chair. Uh, Minister, in the, um, in your annual report for 2024/2025 after priority one which is about economic growth and productivity is improving road safety- Yes.

I'm just, uh, curious on whether, um, both priorities can go hand-in-hand in your view and if so, what has your ministry done in the last year to improve road safety and what are the results? Um... Yeah, I mean, obviously, um, I mean, I think they can go hand-in-hand obviously. We want a, um, a growth enhancing transport system but we also want it to be safe.

That's, that's, uh, obviously important. Um, so w- we're making good progress. I mean obviously, uh, probably the biggest thing is, is the...... Parliament's third attempt to roll out a, um, roadside drug, (laughs) um, oral fluid drug testing regime. Um, so we've now approved the devices and, um, we're doing a launch on the 15th of December, which is not far away actually, now I think about it. Uh, so that's— that's good progress.

I'm— I'm cautiously optimistic about it. Um, uh, the police have significantly lifted their breath testing numbers, uh, with the caveat obviously around the, um, uh, the news that I think everyone's aware of around, um, some of the discrepancies, um, which is of concern to me obviously, and there's a process underway around that.

Um, but notwithstanding, uh, that discrepancy there has been a- an uplift and certainly anecdotally I get- I get quite a bit of feedback from people that they're noticing more breath testing, um, happening, which- which is in and of itself is quite an important thing because, you know, people- people think that they might get, um, breath tested on the way home, they- they think again about that extra drink or stuff, so that- that does make a difference.

Um, so we're making good progress, we're not there yet, um, the road toll- road toll is too high, I want to see it come down obviously. Mm-hmm. Um, yeah. Good. Thank you. Julianne. Um, just coming back to the roads of national significance, and you mentioned the trade-offs with other parts of, um, the Crown's investment in hospitals or other types of infrastructure. Um, the Infrastructure Commission said earlier this week to us at this committee that they had- that they had not received, uh, the roads of national significance

projects, um, as part of the IPP assessment, which is of course a- a, um, a really excellent, um, system that your- your government and you have initiated to identify project readiness and- and improve investment. Given the, like, large amount of likely Crown money that's going to be going into some of these- Mm-hmm.

... will- can we expect them to be subject to the same process other infrastructure projects are? Uh, po- possibly. Um, ca- a couple of things I'd say is, one is the IPP program is- is in its- in its infancy, so we've just had the release of the Round Two, uh, results and it's- it's currently a voluntary program and that's- that's partly deliberate because we want to, you know, get some runs on the board and see how it goes, and feedback is it's- is it's pretty good. It's not so much about project readiness, in fact

it's almost the opposite, it's more about, um, you know, well I mean stage one, the- the first gateway is, is there a problem? You know? And- and actually in and of itself that's- that's quite a useful kind Mm-hmm.... thought process to make people go through, like, is there a problem here? Is the- is the solution you've come up with actually a good one? And then is it deliverable? So, and- and nothing's got actually stage three approval yet, in fact most- most people apply at stage two through the IPP because they think they've got this great idea and actually they've been told,

"Well, we're not sure about that as the solution, but you've identified a problem, so go back and do some extra work on..." You know, and- and that analytical rigor is- is good, is a good thing. So, but it's in its infancy, we're- it's not even a year old. And NZTA of course has its, which you can go through with them, you know, they've got their own... I mean it's a Crown agency isn't it, they've got their own approval processes around, um, uh, delivery and readiness and value for money and- and all that stuff.

So, um, they're kind of separate processes and, uh, in time we may- we may get to a position where- where- where there's- there's some involvement there. Um, but you know, not every Crown agency is going through the IPP process at the moment, it's a voluntary thing, Defense has put some stuff in from memory, um, but Education hasn't, um, Health hasn't. There might be, there might be a couple of Health ones, but yeah.
